name: Vía Verde del Trenillo Loop
name_original: La Puebla de Almoradiel, Quintanar de la Orden y Villa de Don Fadrique – vuelta en la Vía Verde del Trenillo
description: Cycle the moderate 27.0-mile Vía Verde del Trenillo Loop through wetlands, vineyards, and olive groves in the Toledo plain.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around La Puebla de Almoradiel are characterized by predominantly flat terrain, making the region accessible for various skill levels. The landscape features expansive agricultural fields, including vineyards and olive groves, alongside the scenic Cigüela River. This area offers a network of paths, some of which are dedicated bike lanes, providing smooth journeys through the natural environment. Best touring cycling routes around La Puebla De Almoradiel The most popular touring cycling route is La Mancha Wetlands Loop, a 38.1 miles (61.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 59 minutes to complete. This route navigates through the region's wetlands and agricultural areas. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Vía Verde del Trenillo Loop, a moderate 27.0 miles (43.5 km) path. This trail follows a section of the Vía Verde El Trenillo, offering a dedicated cycling experience through the flat plains. Local touring cyclists also love the Quintanar de la Orden – Church of Santiago Apóstol loop from Quintanar de la Orden, a 13.4 miles (21.5 km) trail leading through agricultural fields and local towns, often completed in about 1 hour 46 minutes. Touring cycling around La Puebla De Almoradiel is defined by flat plains, extensive agricultural fields, and riverside paths along the Cigüela River. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging long-distance routes. The routes in La Puebla De Almoradiel are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 4 reviews. More than 30 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore La Puebla De Almoradiel's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general terrain like for touring cycling around La Puebla de Almoradiel?
A: The region around La Puebla de Almoradiel is predominantly flat, making it highly accessible and comfortable for touring cyclists of various skill levels. Routes often combine traditional paths with dedicated bike lanes, traversing expansive agricultural fields, vineyards, and olive groves, as well as scenic riverside paths along the Cigüela River.

Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in the La Puebla de Almoradiel area?
A: There are over 25 touring cycling routes available around La Puebla de Almoradiel, catering to different preferences and abilities. You'll find a good mix of easy, moderate, and more challenging long-distance options.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in La Puebla de Almoradiel?
A: Yes, the generally flat terrain makes many routes suitable for families. For an easier option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38212245" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quintanar de la Orden – Church of Santiago Apóstol loop from Quintanar de la Orden&lt;/a&gt;, which is 13.4 miles (21.5 km) and leads through agricultural fields and local towns.

Q: What natural features can I expect to see while cycling in this region?
A: Cyclists can enjoy diverse natural features, including the picturesque Cigüela River and its wetlands, which are home to rich birdlife and flora. The routes also pass through vast agricultural landscapes, characterized by vineyards, olive groves, and cereal fields, creating a 'green sea' effect, especially vibrant in autumn. The area is also known for its clean skies, making it ideal for stargazing.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops for convenience. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022692873/via-verde-del-trenillo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde del Trenillo Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a dedicated cycling experience through flat plains, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022588258/la-mancha-wetlands-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Mancha Wetlands Loop&lt;/a&gt; navigates through wetlands and agricultural areas.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I explore along the cycling routes?
A: While cycling, you can encounter several historical and cultural sites. Notable attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5441399" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parish Church of San Juan Bautista&lt;/a&gt; in La Puebla de Almoradiel, and various hermitages. The region also features traditional wells, such as the 'Pozo de las Cuatro Bocas', and has archaeological significance from Iberian and Celtic periods.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in La Puebla de Almoradiel?
A: The touring cycling routes in La Puebla de Almoradiel are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 4 reviews. Cyclists often praise the tranquil environment, the flat and accessible terrain, and the diverse scenery of agricultural fields and riverside paths.

Q: Is there a dedicated greenway for cycling in the area?
A: Yes, a notable option is a section of the Vía Verde El Trenillo. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022692873/via-verde-del-trenillo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde del Trenillo Loop&lt;/a&gt; utilizes this greenway, providing a smooth and dedicated cycling path through the flat plains.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in La Puebla de Almoradiel?
A: The region is appealing for cycling throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn offer particularly pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes, with autumn showcasing striking colors in the agricultural fields. The flat terrain also makes it manageable during milder winter days.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by windmills or other iconic La Mancha scenery?
A: Yes, you can find routes that incorporate the iconic La Mancha windmills. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20135872" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Windmills – Statue of Quixote and Dulcinea loop from La Puebla de Almoradiel&lt;/a&gt; is a longer route that takes you past these famous landmarks.

Q: Is parking available near the starting points of cycling routes?
A: While specific parking areas for each route are not detailed, La Puebla de Almoradiel and surrounding towns generally offer public parking options. It's advisable to check local parking availability in the town centers or near known trailheads before your ride.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the touring cycling routes?
A: Many outdoor areas in La Puebla de Almoradiel, particularly the agricultural paths and riverside trails, are generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through cultivated fields or near wildlife habitats, and to respect local regulations.
tips:
La Puebla de Almoradiel: Its origins are linked to an Iberian and Celtic fortress, inhabited by Romans, Visigoths, and, during the Muslim era, by the Almoravids.
The ancient city stood on the Cigüela River, but was abandoned in 1276 due to epidemics.
Repopulated by the Order of Santiago in the Middle Ages, it received a town charter in 1315 and the privilege of town status in 1341.

This beautiful square in Puebla de Almoradiel is one of the meeting places for locals. There are benches, shades and boards to play chess. In addition, you can have a typical tapa from the province in one of the bars that are next to the garden.

Quintanar de la Orden is a charming destination in Castilla-La Mancha, full of history and culture.
Church of Santiago Apóstol: This late Gothic and Renaissance style temple is one of the most important religious buildings in the town. Its two atriums and its robust square tower stand out.
Hermitage of Nuestra Señora de la Piedad: A place of great devotion, this hermitage houses the patron saint of Quintanar de la Orden.
And much more....😎🚵🌄🚴🚶‍♂️

Founded in 1343 by Prince Don Fadrique Alfonso of Castile, Master of the Order of Santiago, who granted the Charter and the Sepúlveda charter.
It was originally called La Puebla de la Isla because it was surrounded by water.
The current name honors Don Fadrique, who took refuge in these lands fleeing from Pedro I "The Cruel."

faqs.question: Is the Vía Verde del Trenillo a loop trail?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde del Trenillo itself is a linear route, following an old railway line between Villacañas and Quintanar de la Orden. However, the komoot tour named "Vía Verde del Trenillo Loop" incorporates this linear section into a larger circular route, allowing you to experience the greenway as part of a loop.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily follows the Vía Verde del Trenillo, which is built on a disused railway line. This means the terrain is largely flat with a very gentle gradient. The surface is mostly compacted earth, making it suitable for touring bikes, hybrids, and mountain bikes. You will encounter four bridges and four footbridges along the way.

faqs.question: What are the key points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several towns and natural areas. You can see the Parish Church of San Juan Bautista and La Femme de la Terre Statue in La Puebla de Almoradiel, and the Plaza de la Constitución. Other notable towns include Quintanar de la Orden Town Center and La Villa de Don Fadrique. The trail also showcases tranquil landscapes of wetlands, vineyards, and olive groves, and passes through areas of the "Mancha Húmeda," a Special Protection Area for Birds (ZEPA).

faqs.question: Is the route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Yes, due to its largely flat and gentle gradient, the Vía Verde del Trenillo section is ideal for beginners and families. The compacted earth surface is stable, and the minimal elevation changes make for a relaxed and low-effort ride.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling. Summers can be hot in this region of Spain, so if cycling then, it's advisable to go early in the morning or late in the afternoon. Winter cycling is possible, but check local weather conditions.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Vía Verde del Trenillo?
faqs.answer: While specific regulations for dogs on this exact Vía Verde are not detailed, generally, dogs are permitted on Vías Verdes in Spain, provided they are kept on a leash and their owners clean up after them. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and does not disturb wildlife or other users.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to use the trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a permit or have to pay an entrance fee to cycle on the Vía Verde del Trenillo. Like most Vías Verdes in Spain, it is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Where can I find parking or access public transport for this route?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde del Trenillo connects towns such as Villacañas, La Villa de don Fadrique, La Puebla de Almoradiel, and Quintanar de la Orden. You can typically find parking in these towns. For public transport, you would need to check local bus or train services connecting to these towns, as specific details for the trailheads are not provided.

faqs.question: Are there amenities like cafes or water stops along the route?
faqs.answer: While specific amenities directly on the Vía Verde del Trenillo are not detailed, the route passes through several towns (Villacañas, La Villa de don Fadrique, La Puebla de Almoradiel, and Quintanar de la Orden) where you can find cafes, restaurants, and shops for refreshments and water. Some old train stations along Vías Verdes are often converted into services, so keep an eye out for those.

faqs.question: What kind of natural features can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The trail offers a serene environment with wetlands, vineyards, and olive groves, showcasing the agricultural heritage of the Toledo plain. It passes through areas forming part of the "Mancha Húmeda," a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve and Special Protection Area for Birds (ZEPA), particularly around lagoons like Laguna Larga, Tirez, and Peña Hueca, offering opportunities for birdwatching.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of the Camino Natural Vía Verde de Villacañas a Quintanar de la Orden o del Trenillo and the Vía Verde del Trenillo. You will also find smaller segments overlapping with the Camino de Santiago del Sureste and La Estela del Gigüela.
